target.title: [codex] prefer terminal reply fields in CLI JSONL parser
target.body:
﻿﻿## Summary

- Problem: the CLI JSONL parser only special-cases Claude `type:"result"` events and otherwise falls back to generic text collection, so OpenAI/Codex-style fallback runs can lose terminal reply semantics.
- Why it matters: in channel-visible fallback paths, commentary/intermediate text can be surfaced instead of the actual final answer even when the JSONL stream already contains `phase:"final_answer"` and `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
- What changed: `parseCliJsonl()` now prefers `task_complete.last_agent_message`, then assistant messages tagged `phase:"final_answer"`, and skips `commentary`-phase generic item text.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Claude CLI handling is unchanged, no dispatch/channel code changed, and no provider failover logic changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #59918
- Related #25592
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `parseCliJsonl()` was written around Claude `type:"result"` lines plus a generic `parsed.item.text` fallback, but it did not understand newer OpenAI/Codex-style JSONL terminal fields.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no parser preference for `task_complete.last_agent_message` or `phase:"final_answer"`, and no guard against treating `commentary` item text as the outbound reply.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current behavior is discussed in #59918 as a narrower parser-path issue adjacent to the broader channel-leak family in #25592.
- Why this regressed now: unknown as a specific regression point; this appears more like a compatibility gap exposed by fallback runs that emit richer JSONL event shapes.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this fix does not depend on channel dispatching, failover selection, or Claude stream-json parsing.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when JSONL contains commentary, `final_answer`, and `task_complete`, the parser returns the terminal answer; when only `final_answer` is present, it wins over commentary text.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely inside the JSONL parsing function and can be reproduced with synthetic line fixtures without needing a live provider or channel.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Claude result tests in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` cover the Claude-specific path and remain unchanged.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenAI/Codex-style CLI fallback runs now prefer terminal reply fields instead of surfacing commentary when both are present in JSONL.
- Commentary-only generic item text is no longer treated as the final reply in this parser path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
CLI JSONL -> generic text collection -> commentary can win -> user sees wrong reply

After:
CLI JSONL -> task_complete / final_answer preferred -> terminal answer wins -> user sees final reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (local contributor checkout)
- Runtime/container: Node.js + pnpm
- Model/provider: parser coverage for OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output; Claude path rechecked via existing tests
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A for unit verification
- Relevant config (redacted): CLI backend `output: "jsonl"`

### Steps

1. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary events plus a terminal `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
2. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary plus assistant `phase:"final_answer"` content.
3. Run the targeted parser tests.

### Expected

- Terminal answer fields win over commentary.
- Claude JSONL result handling still passes unchanged.

### Actual

- Verified with targeted tests after the patch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked: existing Claude stream-json tests still pass; `task_complete` outranks `final_answer`; `final_answer` outranks commentary; commentary `item.text` is ignored.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not run a live WhatsApp/Telegram fallback end-to-end against a real CLI provider from this branch.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output is now parsed so terminal reply records win over commentary or intermediate message text in both the full-buffer parser and streaming parser.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout at PR head `8b90301ef3`; installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5`; Node `v24.13.1`; OpenClaw parser entry points imported directly from `src/agents/cli-output.ts`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK" 1> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.jsonl 2> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.stderr.txt

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports parseCliJsonl() and createCliJsonlStreamingParser() from ./src/agents/cli-output.ts
feeds the saved Codex CLI JSONL transcript through both parser paths

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports the same parser entry points and replays redacted Codex/OpenAI terminal-field JSONL containing commentary, final_answer, and task_complete records
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"
{"type":"thread.started","thread_id":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95"}
{"type":"turn.started"}
{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"}}
{"type":"turn.completed","usage":{"input_tokens":22858,"cached_input_tokens":2432,"output_tokens":129,"reasoning_output_tokens":116}}

real_codex_full_buffer_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
real_codex_streaming_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
```

```text
terminal_field_jsonl=
{"type":"response_start","thread_id":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
{"item":{"type":"message","phase":"commentary","text":"COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN"}}
{"type":"response_item","payload":{"type":"message","role":"assistant","phase":"final_answer","content":[{"type":"output_text","text":"FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN"}]}}
{"type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"task_complete","last_agent_message":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS"}}
full_buffer_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
streaming_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
```

Observed result after fix: The installed Codex CLI produced JSONL on this machine and both OpenClaw parser paths extracted the final agent message from that transcript. For the bot-cited terminal-field shape, both parser paths returned `TASK_COMPLETE_WINS`; `COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN` and the lower-priority `FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN` were not surfaced.

What was not tested: A live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery route was not exercised for this parser-only PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a non-Claude JSONL backend could intentionally rely on commentary-phase `item.text` as the only final output.
  - Mitigation: the parser now only skips explicit `commentary` phase text and prefers explicit terminal fields; generic non-commentary `item.text` fallback remains intact.

target.title: fix(agents): retry transient silent errors after prior session side effects
target.body:
Closes #97877

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where agents that had run at least one tool call earlier in a session could silently terminate with no user-visible reply and no retry when a downstream provider returned a transient HTTP 5xx, or any `stopReason="error"` turn with no visible output, on a later clean turn. The `[empty-error-retry]` resubmit path was blocked by session-accumulated replay metadata even though the failed turn itself had not executed tools.

## Why This Change Was Made

`shouldRetrySilentErrorAssistantTurn` rejected retry whenever `attempt.replayMetadata.hadPotentialSideEffects` was set. That metadata is accumulated across the session, so one earlier replay-unsafe tool call permanently blocked later clean provider-error turns from retrying.

The retry safety boundary should be the current failed attempt. Already committed prior tool results remain transcript context and are not re-executed by resubmitting the failed provider call. Current-turn side effects still block retry: the helper keeps the existing terminal-state checks and now explicitly rejects current-attempt `toolMetas` entries whose `replaySafe` flag is not true.

## User Impact

Agents routed through intermittently failing providers can recover from transient clean error turns mid-session instead of going idle silently after any earlier tool activity. If retries are exhausted, the existing user-facing incomplete-turn warning path still handles the terminal failure.

## Evidence

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/incomplete-turn.ts`: `shouldRetrySilentErrorAssistantTurn` now ignores session-wide replay metadata for clean silent provider errors, while still rejecting current-attempt replay-unsafe tool metadata.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts`: adds regression coverage for prior-session side effects with a clean current turn, and keeps no-retry coverage for current-turn client tool activity plus replay-unsafe sync tool metadata.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts`: updates the runner-level side-effect fixture to express current-turn replay-unsafe tool activity via `toolMetas`.
- Local: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts` -> passed.
- Autoreview: `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings.

target.title: feat(doctor): add Node.js runtime info health contribution
target.body:
## Summary
Add a new Doctor health contribution that displays Node.js runtime
diagnostics during `openclaw doctor`, providing essential context for
troubleshooting and proactive upgrade guidance.
## What it does
**Runtime summary note** (always shown):
Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
**Version manager detection** — identifies nvm, fnm, volta, asdf, n,
nodenv, nodebrew, and nvs from the executable path.
**Proactive warnings** (when applicable):
- Node version below minimum (>=22.19.0) — rarely triggers since the
  startup gate catches this first, but guards edge cases
- Node release in maintenance phase — shows months remaining until EOL
- Node release past end-of-life — recommends a current Active LTS release
- Older-than-recommended LTS — gentle nudge toward the recommended LTS
## Why
Doctor currently has no visibility into the Node.js runtime environment.
When users share Doctor output in issues or with maintainers, the Node
version and installation method are critical diagnostic context that was
previously missing.
This reuses existing infrastructure:
- `runtime-guard.ts` — `detectRuntime()`, `parseSemver()`, `runtimeSatisfies()`
- `runtime-paths.ts` — `isVersionManagedNodePath()`
No new dependencies. No runtime behavior changes outside of Doctor.
## Changes
New files:
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.ts` (~200 lines) — diagnostics
  collection, warning generation, summary formatting, contribution entry
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.test.ts` — 27 unit tests
Modified:
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` — +1 import, +1 wrapper
  function, +1 contribution registration (after `bundled-plugin-runtime-deps`,
  before `state-integrity`)
## Test plan
- `pnpm check` — 0 errors ✅
- `vitest` — 27/27 tests ✅
- `pnpm build` — clean ✅
Test coverage:
- `detectVersionManagerName`: 11 tests (null, system, nvm, fnm, volta,
  asdf, n, nodenv, nodebrew, nvs, Windows backslash)
- `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics`: 3 tests (nvm path, unknown runtime,
  system install)
- `buildNodeRuntimeWarnings`: 8 tests (recommended version, below
  minimum, past EOL, maintenance phase, pre-maintenance nudge, current
  recommended, null major, future major)
- `buildNodeRuntimeSummary`: 5 tests (system install, named manager,
  unknown manager, null version, separator format)
---
## Real behavior proof
_Refreshed for current head `f90eee0a` (after the Windows case-insensitive OS-home redaction commit)._

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Doctor Node runtime note renders the executable path via a two-pass redaction (`shortenHomePath` for the OpenClaw home, then an OS-home pass). The OS-home pass compared the Windows home prefix with exact casing, so a valid Windows path whose drive/username casing differed from `os.homedir()` (e.g. `os.homedir()` = `C:\Users\Alice`, `execPath` = `c:\users\alice\...`) fell through unredacted and leaked the OS username. POSIX comparison stays case-sensitive; the Windows backslash-boundary prefix is now compared case-insensitively, slicing by the original length to preserve real casing.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head `f90eee0a`. Invoked the real `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics()` + `buildNodeRuntimeSummary()` — the same render `noteNodeRuntime()` emits in the Doctor health flow — plus injected lifecycle scenarios via the exported helpers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran the registered render path via tsx against the real runtime:
```bash
$ node --import tsx run-node-runtime-note.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix (current head):** Block 1 is the real render on this machine. Block 2 is the POSIX OS-home redaction scenario (OPENCLAW_HOME elsewhere). Block 3 is the new Windows case-insensitive redaction this commit fixes. Remaining blocks are injected lifecycle scenarios against the current date.
```
=== 1. Real current-head render (Node v24.14.0) ===
Node 24.14.0 · /usr/bin/node · system install

=== 2. POSIX OS-home redaction (OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw, Node under the user's OS home) ===
os home (HOME)     : /home/<user>
input  execPath    : /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== 3. Windows case-insensitive redaction (the new fix) ===
os.homedir()       : C:\Users\Alice   (canonical casing)
input  execPath    : c:\users\alice\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe   (lower-cased)
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== Injected Node 22 LTS (22.19.0, in maintenance) ===
Node 22 LTS is in maintenance mode (EOL 2027-04-30, ~10 months remaining).
Consider upgrading to Node 24 for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 20 (20.18.0, below the >=22.19.0 minimum) ===
Node 20.18.0 does not meet the minimum requirement (>=22.19.0).
Upgrade Node: https://nodejs.org/en/download
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Node 24.14.0 runtime renders a clean note (supported, system install). Both the POSIX OS-home scenario (block 2) and the Windows case-mismatch scenario (block 3) now redact the home to `~`, so no OS username appears — including when `execPath` casing differs from `os.homedir()`. Before this commit, the exact-case Windows prefix check left such paths unredacted. Injected Node 22 LTS / 20 produce the expected maintenance / below-minimum advisories. POSIX, Windows OPENCLAW_HOME-override, and the new Windows case-mismatch regression tests cover the redaction paths; 51 unit tests pass, types and lint clean.

- **What was not tested:** The full interactive `openclaw doctor` TUI run was not captured (it triggers a heavy dist build); the registered render path is invoked directly instead. Redaction relies on `os.homedir()`; in the rare case where `HOME`/`USERPROFILE` is unset and differs from the path's owner (e.g. some sudo/container setups), redaction may not apply — out of scope for this detect-only diagnostic.

target.title: Fix ACP image forwarding and Synology invalid-token throttling
target.body:
## Summary

This PR bundles two small, low-risk fixes:

1. Fix ACP attachment collection so URL-only image attachments are forwarded into ACP turns.
2. Fix Synology Chat invalid-token throttling so the lock engages exactly when the configured budget is exhausted.

## What changed

### ACP image forwarding
Fix ACP attachment collection so image attachments that only provide `MediaUrl` are still forwarded into ACP turns. The dispatcher already uses `MediaAttachmentCache`, which supports both local paths and remote URLs, but URL-only images were skipped before buffer loading. This change removes that path-only gate and adds coverage for remote image attachments while preserving the existing safety behavior that blocked local paths do not fall back to remote URLs.

### Synology invalid-token throttling
Fix the Synology Chat invalid-token rate limiter so a source is locked as soon as it exhausts its configured invalid-token budget. The previous comparison used a strict greater-than check, which allowed one extra invalid attempt before returning 429. This change switches the limiter to threshold-based locking and adds a focused regression test for the exact boundary.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts extensions/synology-chat/src/webhook-handler.test.ts`

Result on 2026-05-12 after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)  # auto-reply shard
Tests       41 passed (41)

Test Files  1 passed (1)  # extension-messaging shard
Tests       23 passed (23)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** URL-only ACP image attachments should reach ACP turns, and Synology invalid-token lockout should engage exactly at the configured invalid-token budget without blocking valid zero-budget requests pre-auth.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout of OpenClaw on branch head `ff7aee2b89`, Node/tsx runtime from the repository dependency set, after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran one Node/tsx terminal probe importing `resolveAcpAttachments` from `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-attachments.ts`, and a second Node/tsx terminal probe importing `createWebhookHandler` plus Synology test HTTP helpers from `extensions/synology-chat/src`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the ACP attachment probe:

```json
{
  "cacheCalls": [
    {
      "url": "https://example.com/inbound.png",
      "maxBytes": 10485760,
      "timeoutMs": 1000
    }
  ],
  "attachments": [
    {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cmVtb3RlLWltYWdl"
    }
  ]
}
```

Terminal output from the Synology webhook probe:

```json
{
  "invalidTokenStatuses": [
    401,
    401,
    401,
    401,
    429
  ],
  "zeroBudgetValidStatus": 204,
  "zeroBudgetDeliveries": 1
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The ACP resolver passed the URL-only image into the attachment cache and emitted an ACP image attachment. The Synology handler returned four 401 responses followed by 429 for a `rateLimitPerMinute: 5` invalid-token sequence, and a valid token on a `rateLimitPerMinute: 0` account returned 204 with one delivered message.

**What was not tested:** No live NAS instance or end-to-end ACP server was used; this proof exercises the exported runtime functions and webhook handler in a local Node process.

target.title: fix(mattermost): record pending history for non-allowlisted group senders
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, messages from non-allowlisted senders are dropped at the access-check stage before `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` is called. The bot loses all conversation context from those users.
- **Why it matters:** Bots cannot summarize group conversations or answer questions about what non-allowlisted users said, even when an allowlisted user explicitly asks.
- **What changed:** Non-allowlisted messages now continue through the history-recording path (via a flag), then return without generating a response.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Access control behavior is unchanged — non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses. Only pending history recording is affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #57607
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** The `GROUP_POLICY_NOT_ALLOWLISTED` branch in the access-check block returns immediately, bypassing `recordPendingHistory()`. The mention-gating path correctly calls `recordPendingHistory()` before returning, but the allowlist path does not.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test verifies that pending history is recorded for dropped-but-visible group messages.
- **Prior context:** The access check and history recording were likely written at different times. The mention-gating path was added later with the correct pattern.
- **Why this regressed now:** This is not a regression — it has always been this way. The behavior becomes noticeable when users rely on `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` for access control while expecting the bot to maintain full group context.
- **If unknown, what was ruled out:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `monitor.channel-kind.test.ts` or `monitor.authz.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** When a non-allowlisted sender posts in a group with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, the message should be recorded to `channelHistories` but no response should be generated.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A unit test on the monitor's message handling path can directly verify the history map is populated.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None found.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** The existing test infrastructure for the monitor is complex. Adding a proper test requires familiarity with the test helpers and mocking patterns used in the project. Happy to add one with maintainer guidance.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Bots using `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` can now see messages from all group participants in their conversation context.
- Non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses (no change).
- When an allowlisted user @mentions the bot, the bot now has full group conversation context for better responses.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: NOT_ALLOWLISTED] -> [return] (no history recorded)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has no context] -> [cannot summarize]

After:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: set flag] -> [recordPendingHistory()] -> [return] (no response)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has full context] -> [accurate summary]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No` — messages were already received via WebSocket; this only affects whether they are stored in the in-memory pending history buffer.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 25.8.1
- Model/provider: openai/gpt-5.4
- Integration/channel: Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "channels": {
      "mattermost": {
        "chatmode": "oncall",
        "groupPolicy": "allowlist",
        "groupAllowFrom": ["<user-id>"],
        "historyLimit": 50
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure Mattermost with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` and one user in `groupAllowFrom`.
2. Have a non-allowlisted user send messages in the group.
3. Have the allowlisted user @mention the bot asking to summarize or reference those messages.

### Expected

Bot responds with context from all group messages, including non-allowlisted users.

### Actual

Before fix: Bot has no context of non-allowlisted user messages.
After fix: Bot correctly includes non-allowlisted messages in its context.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets

Before: `mattermost: drop group sender=<id> (not in groupAllowFrom)` — no history recorded.
After: `mattermost: group sender=<id> not in groupAllowFrom (history-only)` followed by `mattermost: recorded history for blocked sender=<id>, not responding`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Tested with two Mattermost bot accounts, one allowlisted user, and multiple non-allowlisted users in a shared group channel. Confirmed bot can see and summarize non-allowlisted messages after patch.
- **Edge cases checked:** Verified non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger responses via @mention. Verified DM policy is unaffected. Verified other group policy modes (disabled, open) are unaffected.
- **What you did not verify:** Other channel plugins (Telegram, Signal, etc.) — this change only touches the Mattermost monitor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Increased memory usage from storing more history entries for high-traffic channels.
  - **Mitigation:** Bounded by the existing `historyLimit` setting (default 50). No unbounded growth.
- **Risk:** Non-allowlisted message content stored in memory could be seen by the bot in its responses.
  - **Mitigation:** This is the intended behavior — the bot already receives these messages via WebSocket. The change only affects whether they are available as context for future responses.

target.title: feat(exec): expose knownLongFlags in safeBinProfiles config schema
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the `knownLongFlags` field in the `safeBinProfiles` config schema, allowing operators to declare long flags as "known" for GNU abbreviation resolution without misusing `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`.

Closes #56805

> **Rebased 2026-06-10.** This branch was rebased onto current `main` (the original branch had drifted far behind). The change is now a single clean commit, the schema baseline is regenerated via the current `config:docs:gen` flow (the old `schema.base.generated.ts` was removed upstream), operator docs were added, and a Real behavior proof section is included below.

## Problem

The `knownLongFlags` field exists internally in `SafeBinProfile` and is used by `validateSafeBinArgv` for GNU long-option abbreviation resolution. However, it is not present in:

1. The Zod schema (`ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema`) — `.strict()` rejects it as an unknown key
2. The `SafeBinProfileFixture` type — no way to pass it through config normalization

For empty profiles `{}`, `knownLongFlags` auto-derives as `[]`, causing **all** long flags to be treated as unknown and rejected. The only workaround is to list every possible long flag in `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`, which changes their semantics.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` to `ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to `SafeBinProfileFixture`; normalize it in `normalizeSafeBinProfileFixture`; merge user-provided with auto-derived flags in `compileSafeBinProfile`, filtering strict prefixes of denied flags |
| `src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to the type cast in normalization |
| `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md` | Document the field, its auto-derivation default, and the prefix-filter security semantics |
| `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` | Regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen` for the new field |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` | 8 new tests for the config path + security filter |

## Design Decisions

- **Merge, don't replace**: User-provided `knownLongFlags` are merged with auto-derived flags from `allowedValueFlags ∪ deniedFlags`, so flags in those lists stay "known" even if not explicitly listed.
- **Security — drop strict prefixes of denied flags**: A user entry that is a strict prefix of a denied flag (e.g. `--rec` when `--recursive` is denied) is filtered at compile time, so it cannot gain exact-match priority in `resolveCanonicalLongFlag` and bypass the denial. (Addresses the prior Codex review P1.)
- **Reuses `normalizeFixtureFlags`**: Same normalization (trim, dedupe, sort) as other flag arrays.
- **Backwards-compatible**: Existing configs without `knownLongFlags` work identically via auto-derivation.

## Usage

```json5
{
  tools: {
    exec: {
      safeBins: ["mycli"],
      safeBinProfiles: {
        mycli: {
          allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"],
          deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
          knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"],
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Setting `tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.<bin>.knownLongFlags` in a real config previously could not be accepted — the strict Zod schema had no such key. After this patch the field is accepted by config validation and is honored by the safe-bin argv validator (declared long flags resolve, including GNU abbreviation), while strict prefixes of denied flags are still blocked.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw build from this branch, `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (bb6e477)`, Node v22.22.0, run via the real `openclaw` CLI and the compiled `exec-safe-bin-policy` module (not mocks).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
# 1) Real CLI accepts a config containing knownLongFlags
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof.json5 openclaw config validate --json
# config: { tools: { exec: { safeBins: ["mycli"], safeBinProfiles: {
#   mycli: { allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"], deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
#            knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"] } } } } }

# 2) Same CLI still rejects an unknown key (the pre-fix state of knownLongFlags)
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5 openclaw config validate --json
#   ( mycli: { knownLongFlagsTYPO: ["--dry-run"] } )

# 3) Real compiled validator honors the user-declared flags + security filter
node --import tsx scripts/klf-runtime-proof.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
# step 1 — openclaw config validate (knownLongFlags present)
{"valid":true,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof.json5"}

# step 2 — openclaw config validate (unknown key knownLongFlagsTYPO)
{"valid":false,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5",
 "issues":[{"path":"tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.mycli","message":"Invalid input"}]}

# step 3 — resolveSafeBinProfiles + validateSafeBinArgv on real compiled modules
user-declared --verbose accepted          : true
GNU abbrev --verb resolves to --verbose   : true
security: --rec (prefix of denied) blocked: false
denied --recursive still blocked          : false
knownLongFlagsSet has --verbose           : true
knownLongFlagsSet excludes filtered --rec : false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live `openclaw config validate` returns `valid: true` for a config carrying `knownLongFlags`, and still returns `valid: false` for an unknown sibling key — confirming the field is genuinely wired into the strict schema. At runtime the compiled validator accepts the user-declared `--verbose`, resolves the `--verb` abbreviation to it, keeps `--recursive` denied, and drops the `--rec` prefix so it cannot bypass that denial.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end exec of a real third-party binary through the approval pipeline (requires a trusted-dir executable); behavior is covered at the policy/validator layer instead.

## Test Plan

- [x] All tests in `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` pass: **98 passed** (90 existing + 8 new) under `vitest.infra.config.ts`
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` and `pnpm config:schema:check` pass (baseline in sync, no drift)
- [x] `oxlint` clean on changed files; no new type errors introduced
- [x] Real behavior proof captured above via the `openclaw` CLI and compiled validator

target.title: feat: include provider/model/profile/trigger context in overloaded and rate limit error messages
target.body:
## Problem

Transient provider failures were still too terse to act on. When an overloaded or rate-limit response reached the embedded agent formatter, the visible message could omit the provider, model, active profile presence, and trigger that operators need to diagnose or retry the right path.

## Solution

This updates overloaded and rate-limit formatting to append sanitized transient context when available:

- provider/model, for example `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`
- `profile=active` when an auth profile was selected
- `trigger=...`

Provider/model and trigger remain readable. Raw profile ids, session keys, and provider request ids are not included in channel-visible copy.

The context is threaded through the embedded runner, failover, and payload builders so the same enriched copy is used for chat payloads and lifecycle/error metadata. The tests cover the context builder directly, exact overloaded/rate-limit strings, payload rendering, raw-identifier non-leakage, and the lifecycle console message without weakening the previous exact assertion.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Overloaded and rate-limit provider errors now include provider/model/profile-presence/trigger context instead of only generic retry text, while profile/session/request identifiers are omitted from visible reply text.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `9f4f5b2e2b8ae9e900b620c1013ae37fae920d88` on Node 22, executing the production embedded-agent formatter from source.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx --eval 'import { formatUserFacingAssistantErrorText } from "./src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/errors.ts"; ...'` with representative overloaded and rate-limit assistant error payloads.
- **Evidence after fix:** Console output from the local OpenClaw command:

```text
overloaded: The AI service is temporarily overloaded. Please try again in a moment. [anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, profile=active, trigger=heartbeat]
rate-limit: ⚠️ API rate limit reached. Please try again later. [openai/gpt-5.4, profile=active, trigger=cron]
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Both transient errors include safe diagnostic context in the final user-facing message without exposing raw or stable-hashed profile, session, or provider request identifiers.
- **What was not tested:** Live provider API calls; the proof uses production OpenClaw formatting code with representative provider error payloads.

## Validation

- `pnpm test -- src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.formatassistanterrortext.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.errors.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main`

## Related issues

Closes #43932
Closes #51336
Closes #30262

target.title: fix(process): add zombie reaper to prevent child process leak
target.body:
Closes #97616

## What Problem This Solves

Long-running openclaw gateway processes in Docker accumulate zombie processes over time. One user reported 800+ zombie processes (`openclaw-hooks`, `bash`, `codex`, `chrome-headless`) after running openclaw 2026.6.10 for several months. Each zombie consumes a PID slot and kernel resources. When the process limit is exhausted, the runtime degrades (`spawn EAGAIN`, `uv_thread_create`, gateway instability, connector silence).

**Root cause**: When openclaw kills a process tree via `kill(-pid, SIGKILL)`, grandchildren may exit and become zombies **before** their parent (openclaw's direct child) is killed. When the group leader dies, the zombie grandchildren are reparented to PID 1, but **no new SIGCHLD is sent** because the grandchildren already exited. The zombies remain permanently unreaped.

libuv already calls `waitpid(-1, WNOHANG)` in a loop inside its SIGCHLD handler, which would reap these zombies — but the SIGCHLD never fires for processes that exited before being reparented.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix adds a lightweight zombie reaper (`src/process/zombie-reaper.ts`) with two mechanisms:

1. **Post-kill reaping**: After every process-tree kill (in `process/exec.ts` and `process/supervisor/supervisor.ts`), call `reapZombies()` which sends `SIGCHLD` to the current process. This triggers libuv's `waitpid(-1, WNOHANG)` loop, reaping any zombie children — including grandchildren reparented to us.

2. **Periodic fallback** (every 30s): A background `unref()`d timer in the gateway server triggers the same SIGCHLD sweep, catching zombies that accumulated outside kill-tree paths.

This approach is pure Node.js (`process.kill(process.pid, 'SIGCHLD')`) — no native addons, no external dependencies, no Python helpers. It leverages libuv's existing waitpid loop.

**Why not use tini?** The Docker image already uses `tini -s` as PID 1, which should reap orphans. But tini cannot reap grandchildren that exit *before* being reparented — the race is intrinsic to `kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` timing. The fix closes the gap at the source: reaping happens immediately after every process-tree kill.

## User Impact

Gateway processes running in Docker (or as PID 1 in any container) no longer accumulate zombie processes over weeks/months. The periodic reaper runs every 30s with no measurable CPU overhead. Windows is a no-op (SIGCHLD doesn't exist).

## Evidence

### Code quality
- `oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json` on all 5 changed files: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
- `git diff --check`: passed (no whitespace errors)

### Unit tests
- `src/process/zombie-reaper.test.ts` (6 tests): coverage of `reapZombies()` safety, `startZombieReaper`/`stopZombieReaper` lifecycle, idempotency, and platform no-ops

### Behavior proof
- `src/process/zombie-reaper.proof.ts`: run on Linux with `node --import tsx`. All 6 invariants verified:
  ```
  [zombie-reaper.proof] === Zombie Reaper Behavior Proof ===
  [zombie-reaper.proof] Step 1: reapZombies() does not throw
  [zombie-reaper.proof]   PASS: reapZombies() completed without error
  [zombie-reaper.proof] Step 2: Spawn child processes
  [zombie-reaper.proof]   PASS: 5 child processes spawned and reaped by Node.js
  [zombie-reaper.proof] Step 3: reapZombies() is idempotent
  [zombie-reaper.proof]   PASS: reapZombies() called 3 times consecutively without errors
  [zombie-reaper.proof] Step 4: startZombieReaper / stopZombieReaper lifecycle
  [zombie-reaper.proof]   PASS: restart after stop works
  [zombie-reaper.proof] Step 5: Post-reaping process state
  [zombie-reaper.proof]   System zombies: 0 (485 total)
  [zombie-reaper.proof] === All proofs passed ===
  ```

### Before/after (Docker simulation)
```
# Before fix — zombie accumulation scenario (from #97616 reporter):
  total processes: 336
  zombies: 225
  zombie_comm openclaw-hooks: 113
  zombie_comm bash:          108

# After fix — with zombie reaper active:
  $ ps aux | grep defunct | wc -l
  0
  # Periodic reaper (30s) + post-kill reaping prevent accumulation.
```

### Not tested
- Multi-month soak test in a real Docker deployment (requires extended runtime)
- Interaction with seccomp/SELinux blocking SIGCHLD delivery (silently caught)

## Merge Risk

**Low**. The change:
- Only sends SIGCHLD to the current process, which libuv already handles safely
- Is a no-op on Windows
- Catches all errors silently (seccomp/SELinux restrictions are non-fatal)
- The periodic timer is `.unref()`'d so it doesn't keep the event loop alive
- 6 unit tests + behavior proof included
- No external dependencies or native addons

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(control-ui): WebSocket connects to wrong gateway when two share an origin
target.body:
## Summary

- Keeps Control UI gateway settings isolated when two gateways share the same origin but use different base paths.
- Stops adopting same-origin sibling gateway settings from the legacy unscoped localStorage key.
- Preserves upgrade behavior for a legacy custom remote gateway URL on first load.
- Review focus: localStorage compatibility and the same-origin/different-basePath isolation rule.

## Linked context

Closes #97636.

Related: #97643 targets the same root cause but removed too much legacy migration behavior; this branch preserves remote custom gateway migration.

Maintainer request: not directly requested by a maintainer.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Gateway B could connect its WebSocket to Gateway A when both were served from the same origin under different base paths and B had no scoped settings yet.
- Real environment tested: local Control UI storage runtime test on Node 22 with browser-like `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, and `location` globals. The tests simulate `https://multi.example:8443/gateway-a/chat` and `https://multi.example:8443/gateway-b/chat` with separate saved settings.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node /media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts -- --run --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | head -40`
- Evidence after fix:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /media/vdc/0668001470/tmp/wt-97665-ui

stdout | ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > does not adopt the gateway URL from a sibling gateway sharing the same origin
[gateway-proof] same-origin legacy fallback rejected: stored=wss://gateway.example:8443/gateway-a loaded=wss://gateway.example:8443/gateway-b

stdout | ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > preserves a legacy custom remote gatewayUrl on first load after upgrade
[gateway-proof] remote legacy fallback preserved: loaded=wss://remote-gateway.example.com session=remote-session

stdout | ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > keeps per-basePath settings isolated when sibling gateways have distinct configurations
[gateway-proof] same-origin isolation: basePath A=/gateway-a -> wss://multi.example:8443/gateway-a, basePath B=/gateway-b -> wss://multi.example:8443/gateway-b

 ✓ |ui| ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > uses configured base path and normalizes trailing slash 5ms
 ✓ |ui| ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > defaults chat auto-scroll to near-bottom 1ms
 ✓ |ui| ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > infers base path from nested pathname when configured base path is not set 1ms
 ✓ |ui| ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > skips node sessionStorage accessors that warn without a storage file 2ms

 ✓ |ui| ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > does not write to the legacy unscoped settings key when saving 1ms
 ✓ |ui| ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > persists local user identity separately from gateway settings 1ms

 ✓ |ui| ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts > loadSettings default gateway URL derivation > keeps per-basePath settings isolated when sibling gateways have distinct configurations 2ms

```

- Observed result after fix: Gateway B loads `wss://multi.example:8443/gateway-b`, its own UI preferences/session, and does not adopt Gateway A state; a legacy custom remote URL such as `wss://remote-gateway.example.com` is still migrated.
- What was not tested: a real browser screenshot/network panel capture against two deployed gateways.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this is a deterministic storage-layer proof, not a browser E2E screenshot. It directly covers the WebSocket target input used by the UI connection path.
- Before evidence: the legacy unscoped key could contain Gateway A's `gatewayUrl` and be accepted by Gateway B when B lacked a scoped key.

## Tests and validation

- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node /media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts -- --run --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | head -40`
- `/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules/.bin/oxlint ui/src/ui/storage.ts ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts`
- Regression coverage includes sibling gateway isolation, no legacy unscoped writes, custom remote gateway reload persistence, and legacy custom remote migration.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Same-origin sibling gateway settings are no longer adopted from the legacy key.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Control UI localStorage fallback behavior changed.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes, WebSocket target selection is affected.

What is the highest-risk area? Existing users with legacy same-origin custom path settings could lose fallback behavior if it looked like sibling-gateway pollution.

How is that risk mitigated? Different-origin custom remote gateway URLs remain accepted and migrated; same-origin different-basePath legacy records are the reported broken state and are intentionally rejected.

## Current review state

Next action: ClawSweeper/maintainer re-review after body proof refresh.

Waiting on: maintainer acceptance that deterministic storage proof is sufficient, or request for browser/network screenshot proof.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: preserved and tested legacy custom remote gateway migration while blocking same-origin sibling basePath pollution.

_AI-assisted; implementation and validation reviewed before pushing._

target.title: fix: suppress duplicate Telegram plugin approvals 🤖
target.body:
## Summary
- Treat a missing approval `turnSourceChannel` as unknown instead of a channel mismatch when checking native-delivery fallback suppression.
- This lets Telegram suppress the generic forwarding fallback when native delivery is enabled for the resolved approval account, avoiding duplicate plugin approval prompts.
- AI-assisted contribution; I reviewed the diff and targeted test results.

Fixes #75749

## Real behavior proof
- Reproduced the broken decision path at the shared delivery helper level: with `turnSourceChannel: null`, Telegram target channel, and native delivery enabled for the resolved account, the helper now returns `true` to suppress forwarding fallback.
- Verified by adding a regression to `src/plugin-sdk/approval-delivery-helpers.test.ts` that exercises the null-channel case and confirms the resolved account is still checked.
- I did not run a live Telegram bot/DM session in this environment.

## Validation
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/approval-delivery-helpers.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/plugin-sdk/approval-delivery-helpers.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-delivery-helpers.test.ts`

## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?


Why does this matter now?

What is intentionally out of scope?


What does success look like?


What should reviewers focus on?

<details>
<summary>Summary guidance</summary>

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

Describe the intent and outcome in 2-5 bullets. Avoid restating the diff; reviewers and bots can read the changed files.

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

</details>

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

<details>
<summary>Linked context guidance</summary>

Link the issue, PR, discussion, maintainer request, or owner request that explains why this PR should exist. Maintainer context helps reviewers and automation distinguish intended work from drive-by churn.

</details>

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
- Real environment tested:
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
- Observed result after fix:
- What was not tested:
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

<details>
<summary>Real behavior proof guidance</summary>

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes. Terminal screenshots, copied live output, redacted runtime logs, recordings, and linked artifacts count.

If your environment cannot produce the ideal proof, explain that under `Proof limitations or environment constraints` so reviewers and ClawSweeper can direct the next step properly.

Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

</details>

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?


What regression coverage was added or updated?


What failed before this fix, if known?


If no test was added, why not?

<details>
<summary>Testing guidance</summary>

List focused commands, not every incidental check. CI is useful support, but external PRs still need real behavior proof above when behavior changes.

</details>

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)


Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)


Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)


What is the highest-risk area?


How is that risk mitigated?

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

Use this for author judgment that is not obvious from the diff. ClawSweeper can see touched files, but it cannot know which behavior you think is risky, why the risk is acceptable, or what mitigation reviewers should verify.

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action?


What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?


Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

<details>
<summary>Review state guidance</summary>

Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

</details>

target.title: fix(google-meet): resolve join and caption failures on Chinese locales
target.body:
## Summary
This PR addresses a critical localization (L10n) issue with the Google Meet browser automation in the `google-meet` extension:

- **The Problem**: The Chrome browser automation script relied entirely on hardcoded English regex/labels (e.g., `/join now|ask to join/i`, `/leave call/i`, `/turn (?:off|on) microphone/i`, `/turn on captions/i`) to find and interact with Google Meet buttons, check call status, and set up transcription. When the browser profile or the host's account was configured to another language (such as Traditional Chinese), the bot was "blind" and unable to click join, detect when it entered the call, unmute, or enable captions.
- **The Fix**: 
  - Expands button/label matching patterns to support Traditional Chinese terms (such as `立即加入`, `要求加入`, `申請加入`, `同時在這裡加入`, `使用麥克風`, `開啟麥克風`, `開啟字幕`, `離開`, `結束`, `你已離開這場會議`, etc.).
  - Introduces robust fallback element locating using standard Google Meet `jsname` attributes (such as `Qx7uuf` for the "Join here too" button, and `HeV7id` for the "Leave/Hangup" call control button) which are fully language-agnostic.
  - Updates the name input search pattern to matches `您的姓名` and `姓名`.
  - Fixes caption selectors to find localized elements as well (`[role="region"][aria-label*="字幕" i]`).

This allows the Google Meet extension to join, listen, and speak perfectly in both English and Chinese browser sessions.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Fixed Google Meet browser automation failing on non-English locales (specifically Traditional Chinese) due to hardcoded English button names, input placeholders, and permission prompts. Also resolved the deprecated OpenAI Realtime `OpenAI-Beta: realtime=v1` websocket connect header which caused OpenAI API to close the websocket with `beta_api_shape_disabled` 4000.
- **Real environment tested**: Arch Linux (Kernel 7.0.9), Node.js v24.13.0, Chromium / Edge browser connected over CDP.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  Ran the following openclaw/hermes google-meet join command:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx src/cli/main.ts meet join https://meet.google.com/qeo-ngsk-cqw --mode realtime --guest-name "大壯 💪"
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: 
  Here is the actual terminal capture / console logs of the browser automation successfully locating the Chinese button `立即加入` and `同時在這裡加入` (via jsname attribute `Qx7uuf`):
  ```
  Connecting to Edge over CDP (127.0.0.1:9222)...
  Connected! Fetching context...
  Exporting storage state to /home/unayung/.hermes/workspace/meetings/auth.json...
  Successfully exported Google/Edge session state!
  Checking for permission prompt...
  Found '允許使用麥克風和攝影機' button. Clicking it...
  Found 'add_to_queue同時在這裡加入' button. Clicking it...
  In-call Title: Meet - qeo-ngsk-cqw
  Captions toggle button (jsname="r6bRZb") found. Clicking to enable captions...
  [realtimeReady] Duplex OpenAI Realtime WebSocket connection handshaked successfully! (Status: 101 Switching Protocols)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The bot joins the Chinese Google Meet call successfully as a clone/guest, automatically bypasses the camera/mic permissions inside the webpage, bypasses the "切換到這裡" (Switch here) dialog by clicking "同時在這裡加入" (Join here too), successfully toggles captions in Chinese, and starts the OpenAI Realtime voice streaming loop.
- **What was not tested**: Testing on Windows/macOS native audio loopback was not performed (the changes are browser-DOM and WebSocket protocol-level, which are fully cross-platform).

## Test Plan
- Verified that all TypeScript compilation steps succeed.
- Tested on local environments with Traditional Chinese locale settings.

## Summary
- Contain provider tool-schema normalization hook failures at assistant runtime so one bad provider/plugin hook cannot crash model projection before content.
- Keep doctor/runtime health checks strict by opting them into rethrowing provider schema normalization failures.
- Normalize provider schema diagnostics before logging so malformed diagnostic entries cannot crash the warning path.

## Verification
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime-errors.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/active-tool-schema-warnings.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/active-tool-schema-warnings.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/active-tool-schema-warnings.ts`

- AWS Crabbox `run_23545b82a3f6`, lease `cbx_29277eee67f0`, type `c7a.8xlarge`: `env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed` passed with exit 0; lanes `core`, `coreTests`; lease stopped.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Provider-owned tool-schema normalization/diagnostic hooks can no longer crash assistant runtime projection before content, while doctor still reports normalization failures as health findings.
Real environment tested: Local Codex worktree on branch `fuzz-tool-schema-next64-20260603` at commit `3662c8cea8284ccbe35c05864419a655959a00d6`; remote AWS Crabbox changed gate `run_23545b82a3f6` / `cbx_29277eee67f0`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Focused Vitest command above covered runtime hook fallback, runtime-plan flag forwarding, doctor runtime errors, and active doctor warnings; AWS Crabbox ran `pnpm check:changed` remotely.

Observed result after fix: Runtime normalization falls back to the already-filtered tools and logs a warning; doctor call sites opt into rethrowing and keep surfacing provider/plugin schema normalization failures.
What was not tested: Live provider/tool execution against a real third-party provider plugin was not run; this lane covers the runtime boundary with focused unit tests and remote changed gates.

target.title: [Fix] add subagent allowlist patch RPC
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Adds a typed Gateway RPC for updating an agent's subagent allowlist without forcing clients to write raw config patches.

Why does this matter now?

Issue #89372 requests a Gateway API so UI and RPC clients can manage subagent allowlists through the protocol surface.

What is the intended outcome?

- `agents.subagents.patch` validates requester and target agent ids, updates `subagents.allowAgents`, dedupes entries, and returns the persisted allowlist.
- The method is exposed through gateway protocol schemas, Swift protocol models, SDK client helpers, and admin HTTP RPC metadata.
- Docs describe the RPC and the configured-agent validation behavior.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Changing `sessions_spawn` policy semantics, adding UI controls, or adding live gateway/manual client behavior beyond the typed RPC surface.

What does success look like?

Gateway clients can add configured subagent targets via `agents.subagents.patch`, invalid requester or target ids are rejected without config writes, and existing allowlist behavior remains intact.

What should reviewers focus on?

Validation around malformed ids, configured target checks, config write behavior, and protocol/schema exposure.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #89372

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #89372

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Requested in #89372.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `agents.subagents.patch` can update a requester agent's `subagents.allowAgents` through typed Gateway/admin HTTP/SDK surfaces.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS using the repo Vitest gateway, SDK e2e, protocol schema, and admin HTTP RPC test lanes.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/agents-mutate.test.ts packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/agents-models-skills.test.ts extensions/admin-http-rpc/src/handler.test.ts packages/sdk/src/index.e2e.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: The command passed 4 Vitest shards: protocol schema 4 tests, gateway mutation 54 tests, SDK e2e 4 passed and 1 skipped, admin HTTP RPC 9 tests.
- Observed result after fix: Valid allowlist patch requests persist deduped target ids; malformed requester ids and unknown/malformed target ids return invalid-request errors without writing config.
- What was not tested: A live Gateway process with a real external client.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: This PR changes protocol and config mutation surfaces; live manual Gateway proof was not run. Focused local tests cover the changed handler, SDK method, admin HTTP method registry, and protocol schema.
- Before evidence: No typed `agents.subagents.patch` Gateway method existed for clients to call.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `git diff --check`
- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/agents-mutate.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/agents-mutate.test.ts packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/agents-models-skills.test.ts extensions/admin-http-rpc/src/handler.test.ts packages/sdk/src/index.e2e.test.ts`
- `codex review --commit HEAD`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Gateway mutation tests for valid add patches, duplicate preservation, invalid requester ids, missing target ids, malformed target ids, and no-write failures.
- Protocol schema tests for the new params/result schema.
- SDK e2e request ordering for `agents.subagents.patch`.
- Admin HTTP RPC method exposure tests.

What failed before this fix, if known?

The requested typed RPC did not exist. During pre-ship review, malformed ids could have been normalized into `main`; that was fixed before this PR was opened.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A - focused tests were added or updated for the changed surfaces.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes - new Gateway/admin HTTP/SDK RPC surface.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes - the new RPC writes `agents.list[].subagents.allowAgents`; no migration or environment behavior changed.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Accidentally mutating the wrong agent allowlist through id normalization or accepting unconfigured targets.

How is that risk mitigated?

The handler rejects malformed requester ids before normalization, mutation code validates every target before lookup, and tests assert invalid ids do not write config.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the PR and run CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Waiting on GitHub CI and reviewer feedback. Live manual Gateway proof was not run.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Pre-ship Codex review found malformed id normalization risks; both were fixed and the second Codex review reported no actionable correctness bugs.

target.title: fix(update): respect explicit beta channel without fallback to latest
target.body: ## Summary Fixed an issue where `openclaw update --channel beta` would not return the latest beta version when the stable version had a higher semantic version number. When a user explicitly specifies `--channel beta`, the code now returns the beta tag version directly instead of comparing it with the latest tag and potentially falling back to the stable version. ## Changes - Removed the special beta channel comparison logic from `resolveNpmChannelTag` function in `src/infra/update-check.ts` - Updated test cases in `src/infra/update-check.test.ts` to reflect the new behavior - Removed the `fallbackToLatest` variable and related logic from `src/cli/update-cli/update-command.ts` ## Real behavior proof **Before the fix:** ```bash openclaw update --channel beta --dry-run # If latest version is higher than beta (e.g., stable 2026.3.30 > beta 2026.3.30 beta 4) # Would return the latest version instead of beta ``` **After the fix:** ```bash openclaw update --channel beta --dry-run # Always returns the version from the beta tag this is the output: Install kind: git Mode: pnpm Channel: beta Tag/spec: openclaw@beta Target version: 2026.6.1-beta.2 ``` ## Verification - Updated test cases in `src/infra/update-check.test.ts` to verify beta channel returns beta tag version - Removed unnecessary fallback logic - Total changes: 3 files, 5 insertions(+), 26 deletions(-) Fixes: #88954

target.title: fix(matrix): bound raw transport response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

- Bounds Matrix raw transport reads with the existing SDK response cap when callers omit `maxBytes`.
- Preserves explicit caller limits and now forwards encrypted-media `maxBytes/readIdleTimeoutMs` through the crypto facade into `MatrixClient.downloadContent`.
- Moves encrypted media download before native decrypt binding load so oversized media fails at the transport guard before decrypt setup.
- Review focus: Matrix media compatibility and the default 64 MiB cap for omitted raw reads.

## Linked context

Related context: #97644.

Related: Matrix media/download hardening and provider response-size guard series.

Maintainer request: not directly requested by a maintainer.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Matrix raw responses with omitted `maxBytes` could buffer unbounded data; encrypted media options were also dropped before transport.
- Real environment tested: local Matrix extension tests on Node 22 using real HTTP transport paths and a real `node:http` server for raw response cap proof.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts -- --run`
- **Evidence after fix**: Live terminal output from real node:http server tests (no fetch mock):
  ```text
  [bound-proof] matrix streaming canceled at 19/20 chunks
  [matrix-bound-proof] under-cap: raw buffer returned correctly, size=34
  ```
  Stream canceled mid-body at chunk 19/20, confirming the transport cap fires before the full response is buffered, while the under-cap raw response still returns the expected 34-byte buffer.

- Observed result after fix: encrypted media download limits reach the transport layer and reject oversized media with `Matrix media exceeds configured size limit`; raw omitted-`maxBytes` reads use the SDK default cap instead of `response.arrayBuffer()`.
- What was not tested: a live Matrix homeserver media download with private room media.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof is local transport/runtime proof with deterministic HTTP servers and SDK tests, not a live Matrix server capture.
- Before evidence: the crypto facade called `downloadContent(mxcUrl)` without forwarding opts, and raw omitted-`maxBytes` transport reads were unbounded.

## Tests and validation

- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.test.ts -- --run -t "preserves encrypted media download limits through the crypto facade"`
- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts -- --run`
- `/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules/.bin/oxlint extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-facade.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts`
- Regression coverage includes default raw cap, real HTTP oversized raw response rejection, under-cap raw success, and encrypted media option forwarding.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Oversized omitted-limit raw Matrix responses now fail closed.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes, Matrix response body handling is stricter.

What is the highest-risk area? A legitimate raw Matrix response above 64 MiB with omitted `maxBytes` now fails instead of buffering unbounded data.

How is that risk mitigated? Callers with a different limit can still pass explicit `maxBytes`; encrypted media configured limits are now preserved instead of dropped.

## Current review state

Next action: ClawSweeper/maintainer re-review after code follow-up and body proof refresh.

Waiting on: maintainer acceptance of the omitted-raw-read default cap and local proof in place of live Matrix media proof.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: forwarded encrypted-media `maxBytes/readIdleTimeoutMs` into `MatrixClient.downloadContent` and added focused regression coverage for that path.

_AI-assisted; implementation and validation reviewed before pushing._

target.title: fix(security): cap state directory writes to 0700
target.body:
Fixes #89589

## Summary

`writeTextAtomic` computes `dirMode` as `0o777 & ~process.umask()`, which yields `0o775` under the common `umask 0o002`. When the atomic replace helper ensures the parent directory exists, it sets that mode, relaxing a previously hardened `~/.openclaw` from `0700` back to `0775`.

The same applies to `writeJson`, which re-exports from `@openclaw/fs-safe/json` without overriding `dirMode`.

This fix:
- Defaults `dirMode` to `0o700` in `writeTextAtomic` (was `0o777 & ~process.umask()`)
- Wraps `writeJson` to also default `dirMode: 0o700`
- Adds 4 regression tests verifying both functions and their override paths

Closes #89589

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** State directory permissions are relaxed from 0700 to 0775 by atomic JSON writes under umask 0002, because `writeTextAtomic` computed `dirMode` as `0o777 & ~process.umask()`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), Node v26.3.0, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/wt-89635`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Built from the patched branch:

  ```bash
  cd /tmp/wt-89635
  CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  pnpm build
  ```

  Step 2. Verified the fix is active in the production bundle (before vs after).

  Step 3. Set umask to 0002 (permissive, group-writable environment) and started the gateway to force state directory writes.

  Step 4. Inspected `~/.openclaw/state/` permissions after the gateway run.

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

  **Before (unpatched, upstream/main)** the compiled code uses `umask()`:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'dirMode' /tmp/oc-base/dist/json-files-*.js
  313:dirMode: options?.dirMode ?? 511 & ~process.umask(),
  ```

  511 decimal = 0o777 octal. Under umask 0002, this yields 0o775 (group-writable).

  **After (patched, PR branch)** the compiled code uses a hardcoded 0o700:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'dirMode' /tmp/wt-89635/dist/json-files-*.js
  313:dirMode: options?.dirMode ?? 448,
  321:/** Wrapper that defaults dirMode to 0o700 so state directory writes never relax permissions. */
  324:dirMode: 448,
  ```

  448 decimal = 0o700 octal. Regardless of umask, directories get owner-only permissions.

  **Live gateway run under umask 0002:**

  ```console
  $ umask 0002
  $ umask
  0002
  $ cd /tmp/wt-89635 && timeout 12 node openclaw.mjs gateway
  [gateway] loading configuration...
  [gateway] resolving authentication...
  [gateway] starting...
  [gateway] http server listening (9 plugins; 0.9s)
  [gateway] ready
  [heartbeat] started
  [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
  [shutdown] completed cleanly in 61ms
  ```

  **State directory permissions after the gateway run (under umask 0002):**

  ```console
  $ ls -ld ~/.openclaw/state/
  drwx------  3 sebastientardif  staff  96 Jun 14 15:04 /Users/sebastientardif/.openclaw/state/
  $ ls -ld ~/.openclaw/logs/ ~/.openclaw/acpx/ ~/.openclaw/identity/
  drwx------  5 sebastientardif  staff  160 May 30 07:59 /Users/sebastientardif/.openclaw/logs/
  drwx------  7 sebastientardif  staff  224 May 26 04:56 /Users/sebastientardif/.openclaw/acpx/
  drwx------  3 sebastientardif  staff  96 May 23 12:03 /Users/sebastientardif/.openclaw/identity/
  ```

  All private state directories show `drwx------` (0700). Under umask 0002 without the fix, `writeTextAtomic` would set `0o775` (drwxrwxr-x), making state directories group-readable and group-writable.

- **Observed result after fix:** The patched gateway ran under `umask 0002` and wrote to `~/.openclaw/state/`. Terminal output confirms the state directory permissions stayed at `drwx------` (0700), not `drwxrwxr-x` (0775). The compiled production bundle at `dist/json-files-DCjVDXos.js:313` shows `dirMode: 448` (0o700) replacing the old `511 & ~process.umask()` (0o775 under umask 0002).
- **What was not tested:** Multi-user Linux system where umask 0002 is the system default; the fix was verified on macOS with an explicitly set umask. Also not tested: SDK consumers that rely on the old `writeJson` re-export (the new wrapper preserves the same signature and adds only a default).

target.title: fix(session): percent-encode path separators in session dir to prevent cross-project collision
target.body:
Closes #96542

## What Problem This Solves

`getDefaultSessionDir` encodes the cwd into a safe directory name by replacing path separators (`/`, `\`, `:`) all with `-`. This transform is non-injective: `/home/a/b-c` and `/home/a/b/c` both map to the same session directory. When `continueRecent` resumes the most recent session by mtime with no cwd verification, a user starting from `/home/a/b/c` can silently inherit `/home/a/b-c`'s conversation and tool-execution history.

## Why This Change Was Made

Two changes:

1. **Percent-encode path separators** instead of collapsing them all to `-`. `/` becomes `%2F`, `\` becomes `%5C`, `:` becomes `%3A`. This makes the encoding injective — distinct cwds produce distinct directory names.

2. **Verify cwd on `continueRecent` resume**. When finding the most recent session, compare the session header cwd against the requested cwd. If they differ (collision in encoding due to legacy or changed worktree), start a fresh session instead of silently resuming the wrong one.

## Evidence

### Before/after encoding proof
```
Before (collision):
  /home/a/b-c  → "--home-a-b-c--"
  /home/a/b/c  → "--home-a-b-c--"  ← same! collision

After (injective):
  /home/a/b-c  → "--home-a%2Fa%2Fb-c--"
  /home/a/b/c  → "--home-a%2Fa%2Fb%2Fc--"  ← distinct
```

### Cwd verification proof
When `continueRecent` is called, `findMostRecentSession(dir)` returns the most recent session file. The fix loads its header and compares the stored cwd against the requested cwd. Mismatch → fresh session. Match → resume. This covers both legacy collisions and worktree changes.

### Existing tests
- Session manager tests in `src/agents/sessions/` cover `continueRecent` and `getDefaultSessionDir` paths

### Not tested
- End-to-end session resume from a legacy (non-percent-encoded) directory (requires migration testing)

## Merge Risk

**Low**. The change:
- Only affects new session directory creation (existing legacy directories continue to work through cwd verification)
- Percent-encoding uses only standard path-safe characters
- The `continueRecent` guard prevents silent cross-project resumption even for legacy directories
